posts.name: Br1an Th0rne
posts.cooked:
I bought a little over 12 cases of ‘19 EP, many in half bottles. My first real foray into EP since the ‘09 vintage; prices got so crazy with the 2010 campaign that I sat out in disgust, and started buying back vintages from ‘82 to ‘00. When a top chateau’s wine in barrel cost more than the same wine from a great vintage that is approaching maturity…well that just didn’t make sense to me. That position flipped briefly with 2019 EP.

In bottle reviews should start coming out soon; Jane Anson (formerly of Decanter) has released her left bank reviews this week, with right bank reviews soon to follow. Her in bottle reports have been encouraging, and I’m quite happy with the purchases I’ve made. Hope I’m still around to enjoy them in the 20 or so years it will take for them to reach maturity; the half bottles should help!

I told myself 2015 was my last futures vintage for Bordeaux, but given the pricing and vintage reputation I had to go for a few boxes of 2019s.

That said, I only bought four wines and focused on things I either have a really strong history with or thought would fare especially well based on the information I had to go on. The low chateau count is also driven by a number of old favorites starting to find their way into the bigger/more extracted side of the spectrum. Not necessarily wines that are going to age poorly, but which are not delivering what I like in a great Bordeaux.

And so my shopping list was Mouton, Palmer, Montrose and Le Pin.

posts.name: Mark Golodetz
posts.cooked:
Before the 2019 primeur campaign, the last time the Bordelais reduced prices was 2008. Decent but not great reviews (apart from Gilman) they were cheap, and hard to sell. Chinese and other Far Eastern buyers entered the market, and they became scarcer and much more expensive.

They also got burnt when they reduced prices so drastically with the 2019 vintage. The early releases were low enough to encourage buying, so later releases came in far closer to 2018 pricing. My point from that long preamble, is that the Bordelais are unlikely to ever go down in price again for a great vintage.

There are still a few wines left over at close to the original release price, and if you have any spare cash, you should be buying the likes of Montrose and Palmer. With prices poised for an increase after a short crop of the 2021s, which I understand to be a tough vintage, backfilling makes perfect sense.

posts.name: Chris_Rickey
posts.cooked: Parker gave the 2008 vintage a good review. From his barrel tasting report: “It did not take me long to realize that the 2008 vintage was dramatically better than I had expected [from reports on the vintage he had heard]. It had all the qualities that make an excellent and in some cases, a great vintage so special: exceptionally dark opaque colors, gorgeously ripe fruit, stunning purity almost across the board, great freshness (because it was a cool year), slightly higher acids than normal, and remarkable density as well as concentration.” Of course, his 2008 review was soon ignored by his report from the 2009 barrel tastings, but it’s misleading to cite Gilman as some sort of lone wolf.

posts.name: Mark Golodetz
posts.cooked:
I bought heavily the 2019 Palmer. Partly because I have always loved the wine, partly because I think the label is one of the most beautiful in all of Bordeaux. But mostly because it was $100 less than similar quality Palmers.

The chateau just released its own notes. I think it makes for some interesting reading.
2019, THE SCHOOL OF NATURE
May 2020 - While January was marked by a few days of extreme cold, the rest of the winter proved relatively mild. Throughout the season rainfall was moderate but sufficient to partially replenish essential reserves in our soils. The vines’ bud burst began almost a week ahead of schedule, but would progress thereafter in a smooth and even manner. The spring that followed was cool and rainy, requiring a high degree of precision in our management of the vineyard. Despite the capricious weather, flowering unfolded without difficulties, fuelling hopes of a bountiful harvest.
Then, at the end of June, the weather conditions would change radically. A lasting period of hot and dry weather set in. Two heat waves between the 26th and 27th of June and the 22nd and 25th of July were fortunately endured without consequence. The limited summer rainfall – 50 mm between July and August – fostered the accumulation of polyphenols in the berries. The grapes underwent their colour change in excellent conditions and by mid-August the prospect of a fine vintage was clear. We began harvesting the Merlot on the 19th of September amidst weather conditions close to those of summer. The berries were concentrated, aromatic and loaded with sugar. The harvest continued and we entered the autumn under fine, steady rain- fall which lowered the potential alcohol of the Cabernet Sauvignon. By the 11th of October the harvest was complete.
A Palmer wine remains the intimate reflection of a mosaic of terroirs specific to this beautiful estate. But it is also the expression of a unique combination of fleshy Merlots and silky Cabernets, complete with a touch of Petit Verdot. In 2019, the Merlots enjoyed dry and sunny weather conditions up to the very end, giving rise to wines full of power, exuberance and flesh. The Cabernets, harvested after the scattered rains of late September, would develop a certain coolness and reserve, to produce wines of rare distinction. The Petit Verdots, for their part, would exhibit remarkable finesse.
Today, both Château Palmer and Alter Ego express a purity and aromatic clarity rarely seen in an abundant vintage. These wines don’t lie; they are remarkably true to the terroir of Château Palmer. And are already part of the rarefied circle of exceptional vintages.

posts.name: Chris_Rickey
posts.cooked:
Dang I didn’t realize how much cheaper 19’s were compared to 18 and 20 after looking at just a few wines.

It depends on what you are looking at. The initial prices on the 2019s were considerably below 2018s and then the 2020s. Many wines that don’t typically sell through as futures remain bargains. However, prices have converged for many of the top wines. In respect of the first growths, for instance, the 2019s are sometimes higher than the 2018s and 2020s. See Mouton, Haut Brion and Margaux, for instance. The “super seconds,” including La Mission Haut Brion and Palmer, are pretty close across the three vintages. The 2019s in Pomerol are still cheaper (I have not checked Petrus, Lafleur or Le Pin, as they are out of my range), although the 2019 Vieux Chateau Certan is rising quickly.

Montrose remains a bit of an oddity. It didn’t release a wine to critics during the futures season, meaning for a long time only two reviews were available (Jane Anson and Jean-Marc Quarin, both of whom have local access to Bordeaux). Without reviews from the main market movers (Wine Advocate and Neal Martin at Vinous), the price hasn’t moved. If it gets a very high score from either, it will be gone in minutes.

2019 pricing really compelled me to get into Bordeaux after mostly being a California and Italy drinker. Not planning on selling anything. Tasted some back vintages, really loved what I tried and jumped in. I went wide across many château but only around 6 bottles deep each and will hopefully benefit from this vintage for decades. My penchant for buying at a discount also got me fired up. It is rare that any asset can be bought at 20-50 percent discount against the likely price 1-2 years out. The number of well reviewed and highly rated classified growths and right bankers that are still available under $60 is pretty amazing. If I were a twenty or thirty something and loved Bordeaux, I’d be jumping in with both feet!

2019 was the first year I did en primeur. As a value buyer, it was the perfect confluence for me: a well-regarded vintage, new cellar space, Covid discount, cash to spend from staying home all the time, etc. As a first-timer, and one that hasn’t much experience in ageing Bordeaux, I spread it out between left and right value plays: Lilian Ladouys, Langoa Barton, d’Armailhac, Branaire-Ducru, Gloria, Siran, Kirwan, Tour Saint-Christophe, Laroque, & Sansonnet. I was happy to see a few of these get mentioned in the 2019 Vinous reports.

The number of well reviewed and highly rated classified growths and right bankers that are still available under $60 is pretty amazing. If I were a twenty or thirty something and loved Bordeaux, I’d be jumping in with both feet!

2019 was the first year I did en primeur. As a value buyer, it was the perfect confluence for me: a well-regarded vintage, new cellar space, Covid discount, cash to spend from staying home all the time, etc. As a first-timer, and one that hasn’t much experience in ageing Bordeaux, I spread it out between left and right value plays: Lilian Ladouys, Langoa Barton, d’Armailhac, Branaire-Ducru, Gloria, Siran, Kirwan, Tour Saint-Christophe, Laroque, & Sansonnet. I was happy to see a few of these get mentioned in the 2019 Vinous reports.

For me as well! Siran and Laroque have made it into my pending list too. Looking at your list you’ve outdone me… Other than Siran and Laroque I added Dalem, Fontenil, Latour-Martillac, Lafleur-Gazin, Pontet-Canet and Montlandrie. Made those choices when I was 23. Now, just 2 years later I would opt for a list more similar to yours. And that kind of worries me, but not as much as it worries my girlfriend.

posts.name: Andreas Scheppler
posts.cooked:
Would you open one over the next few weeks? If so, which and how long to decant?

It’s always tricky to decant young Bordeauxs. Sometimes than can even close down. I would open it a few hours before the dinner, try it, see if its open and ready or not. Some bottles are best not decanted, for some bottles a quick double decant and then back into the bottle for a few hours is enough, sometimes you need a few hours in the decanter (but for a freshly arrived vintage, that last option is usually not the one who I use most).

The 2019 Vintage at its Most Dramatic (60-300 USD):
2019 Haut Marbuzet
2019 Saint Pierre
2019 Valandraud
2019 Tertre Roteboeuf
2019 Clinet

The idea was to single out wines that are rather rich, dramatic, sensual, even flamboyant while remaining, in 2019, beautifully balanced and capable of sustained bottle age.

I tried to do the same in a different register with my “five blue chip classics”, and also with the five bargains and five great values I singled out.

When you publish 400 notes, sometimes it can be hard to break that down for readers, so this was an attempt to make it a bit easier.

posts.name: Sh@n Agra
posts.cooked:
Fair amount of work in this report, when one considers this is the first iteration (so no material here is recycled). I enjoyed many nuggets of information tucked into the notes, including:

Canon: The fact that these vines are now arriving at full maturity surely goes some way to accounting for Canon’s contemporary renaissance.
DDC: The last few vintages of Domaine de Chevalier bear a closer stylistic kinship to the great wines produced at this address in the 1970s and before than they do to the vintages of the early 2000s
Lynch Bages: The last vintage produced in Lynch-Bages old winery, it will be interesting to compare this benchmark wine with subsequent vintages over the coming years.
Montrose: Checking in at 14.4% alcohol (rather higher than, for example, the brilliant 2009’s 13.7% or the 2016’s 13.3%), this is an undeniably powerful, ripe Montrose, but or now everything appears to be kept in check.
PLL: The 2019 has turned out brilliantly, and readers will have a hard time choosing whether 2019 or 2016 is the greatest vintage of the decade at this address: I’ve hedged my bets and bought both.

  A tasting of wines from 2019 put on by the UGCB last November reinforced my impression of the fine quality of this vintage. I majored on the left bank, having covered the right bank more comprehensively during primeurs tastings back in 2020....

Est. reading time: 4 minutes

Reading across a range of sources Marquis de Terme for instance has exceeded most expectations and is offering value for money.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
Fair amount of work in this report, when one considers this is the first iteration (so no material here is recycled). I enjoyed many nuggets of information tucked into the notes, including:

Canon: The fact that these vines are now arriving at full maturity surely goes some way to accounting for Canon’s contemporary renaissance.
DDC: The last few vintages of Domaine de Chevalier bear a closer stylistic kinship to the great wines produced at this address in the 1970s and before than they do to the vintages of the early 2000s
Lynch Bages: The last vintage produced in Lynch-Bages old winery, it will be interesting to compare this benchmark wine with subsequent vintages over the coming years.
Montrose: Checking in at 14.4% alcohol (rather higher than, for example, the brilliant 2009’s 13.7% or the 2016’s 13.3%), this is an undeniably powerful, ripe Montrose, but or now everything appears to be kept in check.
PLL: The 2019 has turned out brilliantly, and readers will have a hard time choosing whether 2019 or 2016 is the greatest vintage of the decade at this address: I’ve hedged my bets and bought both.

Thanks for noticing this! There are over 80 short producer profiles, from one paragraph to three, giving some context on vineyard holdings, viticultural practices and salient details of winemaking and maturation. The idea is to roll these out across the whole region and keep them updated every year as producers evolve.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
I bought a double magnum of the Pontet Canet 2019 as a birth year gift for my great niece. She is supposed to drink it at her rehearsal dinner, so hopefully it will improve. If my nephew gets his way, she won’t be allowed to date until she is 35, and hopefully because of the large format, it will still be worth drinking.

I was a fan of the direction Pontet Canet was going in up to 2011, and tasted the 2010 recently which was showing very well (though a bit of a monster like many 2010s). From 2012, with the introduction of amphorae, the style changed, and my note on the 2019 reflects my unvarnished reflections on the wine. Whether one likes it or not, I think no one could disagree that if you’re buying it expecting to get something along the lines of e.g. Pontet Canet 2010 or 2005, you will be surprised.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
Finally the pendulum has swung. The age of “over extracted” is finally over… and I am glad

Bordeaux has changed a lot in the last decade, and today, what I like to call the “shock and awe” style of winemaking is really limited to a few holdouts who remain attached to the style of the early 2000s. Frankly, I suspect that in many instances these estates’ winemaking teams would like to pull back too, but that it’s the proprietors who are averse to change. Of course, the humbling takeaway is how quickly one goes from being a revolutionary to being a reactionary in the wine world.

Interesting. I am assuming this is a reference to the way Angelus and Pavie have handled recent vintages. I agree with you that Pavie is one of the great terroirs of Bordeaux, and I have not enjoyed most of the wines under the Perse era. I am not sure the Vallettes did such a great job either, and the contrast between the insipid wines of that era and the super extracted of the modern one has meant that this great property has rarely lived up to the terroir. Exceptions 1998, a Perse era pre Rolland wine and the 1964.

Angelus is still modern, but the terroir leaks through. More so recently, as well. I like the direction this is going.

I enjoyed the write up too. I think it has become repetitious to say that they were well priced initially and how quickly they have caught up with other vintages, but the sad truth is that trophy wines are fetching big prices.

But what has struck me is how many wines have scored 94 to 96 points, and among them are bottles which seem particularly well priced. I have just bought bought half bottles of Langoa and Issan in the UK for example.

We all know about score inflation, but it has resulted in a very small spread. It seems that when I looked at vintage charts, a grand cru had to try very hard to score less than 90, and almost every Medoc Grand Cru had a minimum score of 92.

Bordeaux 2019 Left bank

Note that the bordoverview list is still the LPB scores, it has not updated with the WK scores. I think William has done a good job resetting a bit on score inflation, or at least starting to. He took some high-flyers down a few peg e.g. scoring 93 for both Pontet Canet (-6) and Calon Segur (-4).

I did a quick comparison of 70 wines that WK and LPB mutually scored to compare, with the caveats that LPB scores were barrel and she scored ranges (of which I took the mean).

Of the 70, 37% were taken down by 2+ points and scoring below LPB’s range, with only 4% showing a gain of 2+ points (lafite, gazin, lagrange). Other notable wines that saw a drop of 3 or more included Ducru, Pavie, Trotanoy, L’Evangile, and Malarctic.

Again, we are speculating, which I prefer not to do, especially in public, but equally I understand people want to see the reasoning behind the opinion, so… With amphorae, depending on the porosity, you can easily either have a rather oxidative vessel or a rather reductive vessel. If your amphorae are oxidative in their tendency, your wines will evolve rapidly. If you then try to correct that but moving to less porous amphorae, you might tend to have reduction problems—which you would need to mitigate by heavily settling the wine before putting it in the amphorae, and then with rackings with aeration; which could in turn cause other problems. So really you are reinventing the wheel and hoping you will get something that rolls.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
@williamkelley

One note caught my eye which is Lafleur’s release from Fronsac…far from typical Fronsac pricing, but great heritage for such young vines.

Thanks for noticing this!

One challenge is that when resisting score inflation / compression, the alternative that is wheeled out is often scoring “conservatively”, which means in practice never giving 100/100 and never scoring lesser-known producers or appellations highly. What I am trying to do instead is to open up the scale, while using it fluidly to express differences in quality and preference. This wine from the Lafleur stable (actually from just outside Fronsac AOC, though on the same soils) I hope exemplifies that.

If by shock and awe you are referring to the somewhat crude over extraction in the oughts, then yes that is gone over the last decade. But it seems to me (and of course I know far less than you) that the last decade or so has seen a lot of wines continue to get slicker and perhaps more international styled, and we are also seeing alcohol levels increase over recent vintages to levels even higher than the previous decade. Whatever is happening in Bordeaux I wouldn’t call it a return to a classical style exactly

I don’t entirely disagree with you, but it’s also rather more complicated than that. I touch on some of those issues here: Robert Parker Wine Advocate - Unbiased Wine Reviews I’m going to do a big piece on the state-of-the-art of Bordeaux viticulture and winemaking and styles in the near future, but right now I’m in the middle of EP tastings so forgive me if I don’t get into it in detail here!

Fantastic news that you are doing that piece William! Was hoping you would bring the same intense attention to detail to Bordeaux winemaking that you brought to Burgundy. Actually been waiting for a deep dive like that for years, because I know for sure that the wines I am tasting from 2015, 2016 etc. forward are very different than the young Bordeaux I tasted decades ago when I started to collect, but I have no clear sense as to exactly why. “Global warming” doesn’t really cover it.

That piece alone may get me re-subscribing to WA after many years LOL

posts.name: Pat_Martin
posts.cooked:
Funny how the WA’s web interface finally has us getting into the sleepers six days and a couple thousand clicks later…

One thing that stuck out to me - admittedly without plotting the points on a chart - is that William’s take doesn’t seem to line up with the CW that this was a left bank / cabernet vintage. At least for the wines I pay attention to he seems to have both banks excelling at around the same level.

Frankly, reading the specific notes on the 35-40 chateau I usually am interested in across the board (from WK, NM, AG, JA, JD, JS, etc.), this vintage sounds like the second coming, even factoring in grade inflation. Maybe it really is that awesome (and I hope it is), and the likes of, say, the 2019 GPL is indeed a 97 point wine (again, I really hope it is), but I found my doubt-meter triggering as I read through the notes and scores.

For me at least, a big issue is the style of the wines, and the case in point in the 2019 Domaine de Chevalier rouge: the critics all seem to have gone gaga for this wine. I haven’t tried it, but DDC rouge from 2004-2010 leaves me (and many other lovers of traditional claret) cold and it’s all a style issue. I find it glossy, international, anodyne. Did this change in 2019? Had DDC found it’s once-great groove again for a palate like mine? Again, I hope so, but I can’t really tell from the notes.

posts.cooked:
Funny how the WA’s web interface finally has us getting into the sleepers six days and a couple thousand clicks later…

One thing that stuck out to me - admittedly without plotting the points on a chart - is that William’s take doesn’t seem to line up with the CW that this was a left bank / cabernet vintage. At least for the wines I pay attention to he seems to have both banks excelling at around the same level.

Frankly, reading the specific notes on the 35-40 chateau I usually am interested in across the board (from WK, NM, AG, JA, JD, JS, etc.), this vintage sounds like the second coming, even factoring in grade inflation. Maybe it really is that awesome (and I hope it is), and the likes of, say, the 2019 GPL is indeed a 97 point wine (again, I really hope it is), but I found my doubt-meter triggering as I read through the notes and scores.

For me at least, a big issue is the style of the wines, and the case in point in the 2019 Domaine de Chevalier rouge: the critics all seem to have gone gaga for this wine. I haven’t tried it, but DDC rouge from 2004-2010 leaves me (and many other lovers of traditional claret) cold and it’s all a style issue. I find it glossy, international, anodyne. Did this change in 2019? Had DDC found it’s once-great groove again for a palate like mine? Again, I hope so, but I can’t really tell from the notes.

P.S. a big reason I am excited about the notes on wines like the 19 Branaire and d’Issan is I know recent vintages from these estates and I am still a fan of the house style.

For DDC, WK hits specifically on that: “Since 1983, Domaine de Chevalier has naturally evolved: as new plantings came online, the wines lost some of the intensity-without-weight that had always been their signature; in the early 2000s, a concerted effort was made to attain fuller maturity and more concentration, and the wines became a little chunkier and more obviously oaky, too; but recent years have seen a return to seamless elegance, without any loss of depth or persistence. In many respects, indeed, the last few vintages of Domaine de Chevalier bear a closer stylistic kinship to the great wines produced at this address in the 1970s and before than they do to the vintages of the early 2000s. Olivier Bernard and his team, in short, are to be congratulated for ushering in a new golden age at an estate that produces one of Bordeaux’s most singular and characterful wines.”

Edit: Also a reminder, that the WA reviews generally contain overall producer thoughts and updates below that are sometimes as useful as the individual wine notes,

posts.name: M Matthews
posts.cooked:
Good perspective here. I’m glad that the administration doesn’t look at the stock market as the only barometer of success, short term pain for long term gains getting interest rates back to someplace more sustainable. Tech is hurting or bracing for hurt but I think it was the primary winner in the last two years now just reverting to the mean

Bank of America’s CFO said today that consumers are spending and in great shape, loan growth is good, and credit quality is improving. There are no mass layoffs. In fact, as I said, unemployment is almost at record lows. If this is a recession, it’s a Wall Street-only recession, if anything. A recession in a technical sense only.

I know how Wall Street thinking invades the Fed, but if this becomes a real Main Street recession it will be once again Wall Street speculators trying to save themselves. As Cisco’s CEO recently said, “I don’t want to see us talk ourselves into a recession, first of all, and I think people are a little too pessimistic."

Just anecdotally, I’ve been trying to order allocated wines and been denied a few times recently. That means somebody’s ordering them who gets preference over me. That doesn’t sound like a recession.

Based on all that, I stick by my prediction that, save outliers, wine prices will remain steady.

The thing I did not take into account and which could lower wine prices is if China’s consumption numbers remain weak due to its Covid Zero policy. Considering how much wine the French have sent there in recent years, decreasing demand there could have an downward impact in prices here.

Even if it’s a different site, Bellefont Belcier is right between Tertre Roteboeuf and Larcis Ducasse on the same slope with very similar soils, and right there you have three very, very different wines from similar terroirs. And just look at the change at Troplong Mondot from Rolland to Duclos: the terroir didn’t change at all, but the wine was transformed. So yes, I do think that who makes the wine makes a big difference in this and almost every case.

I think the choices of the team are dictated in large part by the terroir. There are exceptions, of course. But as a rule, at the top estates, I find the choices are chosen by what the vineyard and terroir offer.

BB and Larcis Ducasse are close but not really the same soil. You find more chalk and limestone at Larcis, so you get a saltier expression and you find more clay at BB, which makes it a bit richer.

With TSC, most of the wines from those soils east of the village offer a deep, rich tasting experience. The terroir is cooler and there is more clay. TSC. Laroque, Barde Haut, Faugeres, Clos Dubreuil, Rocheyron, and others, while different, are all deep, opulent wines.

I am aware, some folks on here, are not fans of those wines. But I think they are all fabulous expressions of their terroir. That’s just how I see it.

NM pretty much says that about this wine. However if a lot earlier tastes were more open, that is very indicative of sudden shutdown. You can combat by aggressive decant, don’t be shy to hold the bottle upside down in a jug and pour it back in the bottle or second decanter, NM gave another hint with his 2 hour decant. If you can coax some nose out of it and the fruit is all packed in, it may work out great. Or not, such is the afflication.

posts.name: Andreas Scheppler
posts.cooked:
I had the 2019 Figeac this week (as part of a 20 vintage vertical) and it was consensus WOTN.

  • 2019 Château Figeac - France, Bordeaux, Libournais, St. Émilion Grand Cru (11.02.2023)
    20 Vintages of Figeac (1985-2019): All wines tasted blind. A few observations: 1) Compared many other right bank peers, Figeac never fell into the trap of producing very high ripeness/high extraction wines. The low(er) Merlot content plays a role, but the wines clearly speak of a conscious decision of not going there. But in the 2000s the fruit profile is darker and only more recent vintages go back to the beautiful bright red fruit. 2) The hype is real: The level of greatness this winery achieves since the 2015 vintage is stunning. The complexity, delineation, purity and textural depth are miles ahead of what the company produced before. 3) Figeac shows that it is among the 15 best wines produced in Bordeaux today and considering the En Primeur prices, probably only Montrose and Pichon Lalande have an equally good value proposition in this top group. 4) Winner was the 2019 (97pts) with an unmatched purity and depth, ahead of the 2015 (96pts) and a stunning 2018 (96pts) which I thought to be the 2016 (!) which tells a lot about how well they coped with the hot vintage.

    TN: The nose was at first completely muted and only opened up slightly with time. But on the palate, booooom, an explosion of flavors: layers and layers if fresh pure red and blue fruit, minerality, herbs, chocolate, tobacco. Wow. The real deal. The purity is off the charts here, even a step up compared to the 2015/16/18. The structure perfect. Ultra-fine tannins, medium high and so well-integrated acidity, superb balance, airy texture. The winner tonight and a very promising wine.

    Decanting: Not decanted, good from the go. I wouldn’t decant at this point, despite the rather muted nose. (97 points)

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
2016 is super classic, but quite youthfully shut down and will demand some patience. 2020 for me is more typically Figeac than 2019: it’s very deep and incipiently complex, a touch less extrovert than 19 without being austere, and its oak is more completely integrated. 2016, 2019 and 2020 are the three best vintages of the Faye era so far, but 2020 is my favorite.

All the work they’re doing with cover crops and hedging to adapt to the vintage conditions; picking parcel by parcel and keeping those parcels separate in the winery to optimize each; refining cooperage choices; fine-tuning vinification etc etc all show most clearly in 2020.

posts.name: Sh@n Agra
posts.cooked:
I joined a 2019 Bordeaux dinner hosted by @Dinesh_Goyal

I own a lot of 2019, buying as a birth year vintage without much discipline. Generally, I find the vintage to be full of fruit and freshness, and the goal to find wines where the alcohol is less prominent (or the wine is just balanced enough to hide it). I will be selling some wines, finding them to be bigger / higher alcohol than I would like. That said, all wines tasted were all drinkable and expressive in youth with only a couple hours of air; I don’t get the sense one needs to wait 30 years on many of these wines (many should be in a good spot in 20?).

At this tasting, we tried 3 flights of: Cos Estournel / Montrose, PLL / Lynch Bage, VCC / La Conseillate. Wines all were decanted to some degree from a few hours in a decanter to a double decant the evening prior. PLL/Montrose/VCC were generally in the Top 3 ranking for everyone. I’d personally rank them: PLL (96), Montrose (95), VCC (94-95), Lynch Bage (93/94), La Conseillate (92/93)and Cos Estournel (92). Slightly disappointed by the showing of LB which I tasted at UGC, but a consistent showing of PLL (now my 3rd time tasting the wine).

Below are my rough notes. I was drunk by the end, reflective of high alcohols (many wines were 14%+) and my tolerance being lower than I aspire it to be.

  1. 2019 Cos vs. 2019 Montrose
    Cos: Vanilla oak on the nose. Darker black plum, red currant acid. Freshness not as seamless/integrated as the Montrose. Would have guessed higher alc than labeled 13.5%. 92.
    Montrose: Noticeable step up from the Cos. Cool toned dark red fruit with a layer of freshness. Much more precise and balanced than the Cos. Big but not overripe and has that integrated freshness. If you look for the alcohol, it’s there (I would have guessed 14% versus 14.5% label). Well balanced despite the alcohol. 94-95

  2. 2019 PLL vs. 2019 Lynch Bage
    PLL: Cooler fruit than the Montrose and more fruit skin than the Montrose. Less overt fruit and a step higher in freshness. Label says 14% alc, and the alc is noticeably hidden better than the Montrose with more freshness and less fruit. Ballerina like balance with fruit, freshness and tannins and good length. 96.
    LB: Surprisingly drinkable with a dose of fine tannin at the end. Prominent fruit and prominent tannin here, not totally seamless. More burly than the PLL with the tannins having some austerity. Doesn’t strike me as needing 30+ years to hit peak like the 1989 or 1990. 93/94

  3. 2019 VCC vs. 2019 La Conseillate
    VCC: Very expressive, and I’d call this hedonistic if I didn’t try the La Conseillate right after. Big red ripe fruit, plum skin, nice acid, nice tannin in background. Can feel the 14.5% alcohol but not taste it. Lively, vibrant… and mineral. Hard for me to see how it ages (speaks to my lack of experience more than the wine). Not used to such an ebullient VCC and 14.5% at that.

La Conseillate: More oak on palate, noticeably more oak than the VCC which was comparatively elegant. Alcohol present on palate. Bigger red fruit and more vanilla than the VCC. This wine wanted to go to California but just missed the cut off. Another at the table noted I was being too harsh here, and there was a good deal of acid I wasn’t appreciating and it’s just a wine that needs a lot more time. 92/93 for me, but maybe objectively 93?

Has a bit of a green note that I ascribe to the Cab Franc. Chalky, sweetish finish. Fully bodied, and again, quite enjoyable. I just wanted more, expectations being what they are.

I’m around 90-91 points on this. Interested to see where these go, but not excited enough to drop $100 for the next release. Compare this to how positively blown away I was by the 2019 Durfort Vivens, a wine that has so over-performed in this vintage.

posts.name: Vince T s 3 n g
posts.cooked:
Had a nice single blind tasting last night with a few 2019s . Meant to throw in the La Mish but accidentally brought 2 Lynch Bages.

2019 BDX BLIND MINI-TASTING - (3/6/2023)

Wanted to get an early look at some highly regarded 2019s. Blind tasting is always humbling, and this dinner was no exception - 0 for 5! Regardless, the wines were nearly all impressive, with potential to be transcendent with time.

Preparation: Double decanted these 375s a couple hours before dinner. Every one of the wines evolved significantly and improved over the course of 3 hours.

  • 2019 Château Léoville Barton - France, Bordeaux, Médoc, St. Julien
    From 375, Blind #1. is classy, black fruited, and rich. Robust on the nose. Quite deep, with great structure, a dollop of crushed rock, cedar, and oak. Beautiful, but one of the more demonstrative wines of the night. I guess Lynch-Bages (or Poyferre) based on the fruit, and put it in 4th place, a hair behind wine #4 (Cos).

    Quite surprised to see Barton, which I expected to be a bit cooler and chiseled in profile, and which was one of the more aristocratic wines that I tasted at UGC. (95 points)
  • 2019 Château Léoville Poyferré - France, Bordeaux, Médoc, St. Julien
    From 375, Blind #2. Austere to start, but fills out over 3 hours to become fleshy, with liquor like dark fruit; the most overtly savory wine, a hint of 5-space. Slightly rustic tannins, a bit less elegant than the other 4, and the most backwards. I guess Cos. A fine wine on its own, but clearly not at the same level as the other 4 wines, although I'd expect it to improve over time.

    Again, surprised at the reveal, as it was a very different wine from what I tasted at UGC, where Poyferre was elegant and showing more charming red fruit. (92 points)
  • 2019 Château Montrose - France, Bordeaux, Médoc, St. Estèphe
    From 375, Blind #3. An aristocratic wine - very iron fist, velvet glove.
    A fistfull of gravel, #2 pencil, and a strikingly pure black fruit profile that reddens around the edges. Over 3 hours it continues to show more depth, with lilacs, a hint of cassis, and super refined tannins. Restrained and classic. I guess Leoville Barton. By the end of the night, this is running a close second to wine #5. One vote for WOTN.

    Montrose on the reveal, not super surprising. Glad to have over a case of this tucked away. No real sensation of heat, despite the 14.4%. (96 points)
  • 2019 Château Cos d'Estournel - France, Bordeaux, Médoc, St. Estèphe
    From 375, Blind #4. Elegant, mysterious, quite red fruited and perhaps a bit lighter-bodied than the others. I'm guessing higher Merlot content than the other 4. Lovely aromatics - violets, peony, some wet earth, and very fine powdery tannins. I guess Leoville Poyferre.

    An elegant and refined Cos. Neck and neck with Leoville Barton, but the aromatics give it the slight edge if forced to pick. (95 points)
  • 2019 Château Lynch-Bages - France, Bordeaux, Médoc, Pauillac
    From 375, Blind #5. This is the complete package, almost from the get go. A strapping, muscular wine, that is almost painfully balanced in every direction, with taut red/black fruit enmeshed with pipe tobacco and earth, superb depth, superfine tannins, seamless from front to back. The nose is effusive yet totally classy, utterly beguiling, and I want to just sniff this wine for hours. I guess Montrose. Three votes for WOTN (including mine).

    Not totally surprised to find out it is Lynch Bages, and pretty happy about it - since this is one of the wines that I missed out on at the UGC tasting. (97 points)
  • 1993 Von Schubert Maximin Grünhäuser Herrenberg Riesling Eiswein - Germany, Mosel Saar Ruwer
    Wonderful end to the evening. Not super effusive nose to start, just some light petrol and lemon pledge. One sip though, and it's like a lightning bolt in the mouth. A thrilling electric shock of acid, just enough sweetness to tame the edges, with notes of barely ripe apricot, lemon, moderate petrol, pastry crust, and quinine. No botrytis. A finish that goes on and on. Maybe not the most complex dessert wine, but flat out delicious. (96 points)

I attended a blind tasting of the Grand Jury European when the best wines from Napa and Bordeaux of vintage 1995 were compared. The goal was beside the quality of the wines to find out which one was Bordeaux and which one Napa. Nobody was 100% right. posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: 1995 Napa was pretty different to Napa 20+ years later. If you look at the USDA annual crush report, in 1995 the average recorded brix for Napa Cabernet was 23.6 (roughly 14.0%). By 2015, it was 26.1 brix (roughly 15.5%). And of course, plenty of wines are picked above that and watered back.

posts.name: Mark Golodetz
posts.cooked:
Chris_Crutchfield:

Not to open a can of worms, but how many critics actually taste blind though? That would seem to be a key difference for scoring in terms of having access to priors.

Depends.
Most critics are dependent on UGC, and you have a choice of open or blind. Personally, as there was a large percentage of wines that were served non blind, it made absolutely no sense to taste one set of wines blind, and one not.

That is because most top chateaux require you to visit to taste, so when they serve the wines you know exactly what you are tasting. Some of the more influential critics have enough clout to have samples delivered to them to their hotels, and with the help of assistants, they can taste everything blind.

That’s something I always wonder about. As noted, I think earlier in the thread, we’ve all fallen victim to enjoying a big bold wine at the end of a tasting only to have a different experience when drinking it solo. It’s impossible to control for, but I’d be interested in a selected “tasting” of say 7 of these wines over 7 days, with only 1 wine tasted each day. On an island, something that isn’t overwhelming might be rated more highly. Obviously, there are a ton of factors that could skew the data, but the wines could be randomly distributed so nobody drinks them in the same order at least.

I’ve certainly had wines that were near perfect for a moment that don’t really stand out when I try them SBS with something else. It’s impossible to tell if it’s more the moment or the make-up, but that’s why we need to rely on people with tons of experience, I guess.

If I may, the results were in keeping with my expectations. Here you had a group of wine lovers tasting around 50 wines, which I am pretty sure was a multiple of what they normally taste. In addition, the wines were young, a few had closed down, and the better wines were probably showing fragments of what will be their best.

Nothing wrong with this, and I apologize if I am ruffling feathers, but that is a lot of wine and a difficult tasting to boot. I think it is a good data point on the balance sheet, but I am not sure I would want to make too much of the results.

But you have convinced me not to do my 2019 tasting for at least five years.
